Tool_gold_loan_Description: Loan on gold jewellery. 75% LTV, bank vs NBFC rate comparison.
Tool_gold_loan_LearnText: A gold loan lets you pledge jewellery for cash without selling it. Lenders typically fund a share of the gold’s market value (LTV). Banks often price lower than NBFCs; tenure is usually short.
Tool_gold_loan_FormulaNote: Max loan = gold value × LTV. Gold value uses 24K-equivalent weight × rate per gram. EMI uses the standard reducing-balance formula. LTV and rates are illustrative — confirm with your lender.
Tool_gold_loan_ValueBody: Estimated gold value ≈ {0} ({1} g pure equivalent). Maximum loan at your LTV ≈ {2}.
Tool_gold_loan_ScenarioSubtitle: Same gold & LTV, bank vs NBFC-style rates
Tool_gold_loan_ExampleStep_1: Weight 50 g at 22K → pure equivalent = 50 × (22/24) ≈ 45.83 g.
Tool_gold_loan_ExampleStep_4: EMI at 10% for 12 months is computed on that loan.
Tool_gold_loan_ExampleStep_5: Compare a slightly lower bank rate vs a higher NBFC rate for EMI impact.
Tool_gold_loan_Step_2: Multiply by the 24K rate to get gold value.
Tool_gold_loan_Step_3: Apply LTV to get the maximum loan.
Tool_gold_loan_Step_4: Compute EMI and total interest for the chosen tenure.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_Purpose: Estimate max loan, EMI, and interest on pledged gold.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_WhyKnow: LTV and rate decide how much cash you get and what it costs — without selling jewellery.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_RealLife: Short-term liquidity, wedding expenses, business working capital, avoiding distress sale of gold.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_Alt_Emi_When: When the loan amount is already fixed.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_Alt_Home_When: Long-term property finance with tax benefits.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra links gold valuation, LTV, and bank vs NBFC-style EMI in one tool.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_Motto: Unlock gold value — keep the jewellery.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Description: LTCG/STCG on property, shares, MF, gold. Section 54/54EC exemptions.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Subtitle: LTCG vs STCG by asset type
Tool_capital_gains_tax_LearnText: Capital gains tax depends on the asset and how long you held it. Equity often has a ₹1.25 lakh LTCG exemption; property and gold use longer holding thresholds. Optional Section 54 / 54EC-style exemptions can reduce taxable LTCG.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_FormulaNote: Rates and holding periods are simplified planning figures (illustrative post-Budget rules). Debt MF is treated as always STCG at slab. Confirm with a tax professional for your case.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_ExemptionHint: Optional. Reduces taxable LTCG after any equity ₹1.25L exemption.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Status_Loss: Sale is at a loss or break-even — no capital gains tax in this simplified view.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Status_Body: {0}: equity LTCG exemption used ≈ {1}; other exemption ≈ {2}; estimated tax ≈ {3}.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_ScenarioSubtitle: Same prices, shorter vs longer holding
Tool_capital_gains_tax_ExampleTitle: Worked example: equity shares sold after 18 months
Tool_capital_gains_tax_ExampleSubtitle: Buy ₹5 lakh, sell ₹8 lakh, no extra 54 exemption
Tool_capital_gains_tax_ExampleStep_2: Holding 18 months ≥ 12 → treated as LTCG for equity.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_ExampleStep_5: A Section 54/54EC claim would further reduce taxable LTCG.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Step_2: Classify LTCG vs STCG using asset holding rules.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Step_3: Apply equity exemption and optional 54/54EC amounts.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Step_4: Multiply taxable gain by the applicable rate.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_Purpose: Estimate LTCG/STCG tax across common Indian asset types.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_WhyKnow: Holding period and exemptions can change tax by a large margin before you sell.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_RealLife: Selling shares, a flat, gold, or switching debt funds.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_Goal_3: Estimate tax at the right rate
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_Alt_Tax_When: Full-year liability including salary and other income.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_Alt_80c_When: Planning deductions under the old regime.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_Differentiat: FinYatra compares asset types, holding paths, and exemption impact in one capital-gains view.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_Motto: Know the tax before you sell.
Tool_section_80c_Description: Plan ₹1.5 lakh tax saving. Compare PPF, ELSS, NSC, and more instruments.
Tool_section_80c_LearnText: Section 80C lets you claim deductions on eligible investments and expenses up to ₹1.5 lakh under the old tax regime. EPF, PPF, ELSS, life insurance, home-loan principal, NSC, tax-saving FD, and tuition fees commonly count toward the cap.
Tool_section_80c_FormulaNote: Eligible deduction = min(total 80C items, ₹1,50,000). Estimated tax saving ≈ eligible × your marginal slab. New regime generally does not allow 80C — this tool is for old-regime planning.
Tool_section_80c_StatusBody: Total toward 80C ≈ {0}. Eligible deduction ≈ {1}. Estimated tax saving ≈ {2}.
Tool_section_80c_ExampleTitle: Worked example: filling the ₹1.5 lakh cap
Tool_section_80c_ExampleStep_4: Any extra beyond ₹1.5L does not raise the 80C deduction.
Tool_section_80c_ExampleStep_5: Under the new regime, this deduction is generally unavailable.
Tool_section_80c_Step_1: Add all eligible 80C amounts for the year.
Tool_section_80c_Step_3: Multiply eligible amount by your marginal slab.
Tool_section_80c_Step_4: See unused room or amount over the limit.
Tool_section_80c_Info_Purpose: Plan the ₹1.5 lakh old-regime deduction across common instruments.
Tool_section_80c_Info_WhyKnow: Unused limit is lost tax saving; over-investing past ₹1.5L does not raise the deduction.
Tool_section_80c_Info_RealLife: Year-end tax planning, choosing PPF vs ELSS, checking EPF already used.
Tool_section_80c_Info_Goal_3: Estimate tax saving at your slab
Tool_section_80c_Info_Alt_Elss_When: Projecting equity tax-saver returns with lock-in.
Tool_section_80c_Info_Alt_Tax_When: Full old vs new regime comparison.
Tool_section_80c_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra stacks common 80C items against the ₹1.5L cap with a clear tax-saving estimate.
Tool_section_80c_Info_Motto: Fill the cap — don’t overshoot blindly.
